method: 4 decorrelated lenses (code-cite fidelity, dispatch/deadlock, substrate/state-law, spec/invariants) + independent skeptic per finding with no default verdict; mandatory TCC read probe
result: 12 raised, 11 confirmed, 1 refuted; one major (D4 Inv. 9 blindness leak via unconditional LatestOutputPath)

findings.title: Sharp edge 1 cites dispatch.go:362 for the (2a) role map; line 362 is the (2b) arm
findings.reasoning:
The finding is correct as stated. The ADR's sentence attaches the cite `dispatch.go:362` to the phrase "the same (status, scope) table it already uses" in a sentence whose subject is "the (2a) arm". Three checks against the real code confirm the cite is wrong:

(1) Line 362 is not in (2a). Verbatim, dispatch.go:358-363 reads `// (2a) Advance an in-flight refinement first.` at 358, `if t, ok := inFlightRefinement(rec); ok {` at 359, `return spawnFor(rec, t)` at 360, `}` at 361, and `// (2b) Start one on the best non-saturated packet.` at 362. Line 362 is the comment opening the (2b) arm.

(2) The "(status, scope) table" is `spawnFor`, defined at dispatch.go:653 as `func spawnFor(rec Records, t Task) Action {`, which switches on `t.Status` with a nested `if t.Scope == ScopeInitiative` branch at 661. The sole call to spawnFor from inside the at-cap block (opened at 356 by `if occ >= cfg.ReviewWIPCap {`) is line 360, inside (2a). So the line that supports the ADR's claim is 359-360, exactly as the reviewer says.

(3) The misdirection is substantive, not merely off-by-two, because (2b) is the one arm that contradicts the sentence. For initiatives (2b) never reaches spawnFor at all: dispatch.go:364-367 reads `if t.Scope == ScopeInitiative {` ... `return Action{Kind: KindReenter, Reenter: &Reenter{TaskID: t.ID}}`. A reader following the cite lands on the arm that returns KindReenter without consulting the (status, scope) table.

I considered and rejected the charitable reading that `:362` is a loose pointer at the at-cap region as a whole. The ADR's citation convention is line-precise: two clauses earlier in the same sharp edge it cites `refinementStart` requiring `status = 'packaged'` at `(dispatch.go:586)`, and line 586 is exactly `if t.Status != StatusPackaged {`. The ADR hits that cite on the nose, so :362 is a slip rather than a convention of citing regions.

The ADR's substantive claim is true of the code — the (2a) arm does map via the (status, scope) table, and the Editor plan-review arm is therefore reachable at cap — so this is a citation defect only. It does not touch the operator's settled reading-(i) decision, no invariant is at risk, and nothing in D2 becomes unimplementable. Severity is minor rather than major for that reason: the defect is that a reader auditing the deadlock argument during TDD is pointed at the counterexample to the sentence they are auditing. The fix is to cite dispatch.go:360 (or 359-360).

findings.title: D5's domain.Cancel call-site list is wrong and non-exhaustive: mc/verbs/packet.go:60 does not call Cancel, and ~11 other call sites go unmentioned
findings.reasoning:
Both halves of the claim check out against the primary sources, verified independently rather than taken from the reviewer's offered evidence.

(1) MISIDENTIFIED CALL SITE — confirmed. ADR-020:398-401 says "Two existing call sites pass \"operator\" (`mc/verbs/task.go:146`, `mc/verbs/packet.go:60` via `CancelPacket`)". mc/verbs/packet.go:60 is verbatim `if err := domain.CancelPacket(ctx, q, task, reason); err != nil` — it calls CancelPacket, not Cancel. An `actor string` parameter added to Cancel (mc/domain/task.go:421) does not touch that line. The actual second Cancel call site is mc/domain/task.go:451 (`return Cancel(ctx, q, taskID, reason)`), inside CancelPacket's body, which the ADR never names.

Two nuances temper this, both checked: (a) the ADR's "via CancelPacket" hedge shows the author was aware of the indirection — the text is loose, not oblivious; (b) the COUNT "two existing call sites" is numerically correct for production code (verbs/task.go:146 and domain/task.go:451). What is wrong is the identification of the second, and the consequence it hides: CancelPacket's own signature must either gain the parameter or hard-code "operator" — a real, unstated design point.

(2) NON-EXHAUSTIVE — confirmed. My own `grep -rn "Cancel(" mc/ --include="*.go"` reproduced all 11 test call sites (lifecycle_nightly_test.go:1159; initiative_test.go:127,:211; packet_test.go:199; queue_test.go:39,:219; task_test.go:181,:831,:851,:869,:901). ADR-020's "Tests that pin it" (lines 554-584) does not mention re-pinning any — notable since it DOES remember the analogous chore for ADR-008 ("the golden-brief tests re-pinned", line 596). "What gets harder" line 599 ("touching two existing call sites") therefore understates the mechanical blast radius.

DEPARTURE FROM REVIEWER: this is not a design defect. The reviewer concedes as much and is right to. Test call sites are compile-time discoveries — an implementer hits them on the first `go build`, at zero risk. That caps severity at minor; the honest floor is arguably info. Nothing here touches an invariant or the fail-closed posture, and the D5 decision (a parameter beats a parallel implementation that would drift from Cancel's body) stands. Inv. 7's requirement that actor be the logical originator is unaffected. The operator's settled reading-(i) decision played no part in this verdict.

I recommend fix-before-TDD despite low severity for a reason unrelated to blast radius: an ADR's authority rests on its cites being checkable, and a cite naming a line that does not do what the ADR claims is the kind of error that rots — the next reader who verifies it discounts the other cites, which I found careful and correct. The fix is a one-line edit: name mc/domain/task.go:451 as the second call site and state whether CancelPacket gains the parameter or hard-codes "operator".
findings.evidence:
ADR-020:398-401 (verbatim):
"**`domain.Cancel` gains an `actor string` parameter** (`mc/domain/task.go:421`,
which today hard-codes `actor='operator'` in its activity insert). Two existing
call sites pass `\"operator\"` (`mc/verbs/task.go:146`,
`mc/verbs/packet.go:60` via `CancelPacket`); the send-back passes `\"editor\"`."

ADR-020:599 (verbatim):
"- **`domain.Cancel`'s signature changes**, touching two existing call sites."

mc/verbs/packet.go:60 (verbatim) — calls CancelPacket, NOT Cancel:
"			if err := domain.CancelPacket(ctx, q, task, reason); err != nil {"

mc/domain/task.go:447-452 (verbatim) — the unnamed real second call site:
"func CancelPacket(ctx context.Context, q Q, taskID int64, reason string) error {
	if err := requireLivePacket(ctx, q, taskID); err != nil {
		return err
	}
	return Cancel(ctx, q, taskID, reason)
}"

mc/domain/task.go:421,437-439 (verbatim) — the hard-coded actor the ADR correctly describes:
"func Cancel(ctx context.Context, q Q, taskID int64, reason string) error {"
"	_, err = q.ExecContext(ctx, `
		INSERT INTO activity (actor, kind, subject, detail)
		VALUES ('operator', 'task.cancelled', ?, ?)`, taskID, reason)"

My own `grep -n "Cancel" -r mc/ --include="*.go"` (relevant subset, verbatim) — the 11 unmentioned test call sites plus the two real production ones:
"mc/property/lifecycle_nightly_test.go:1159:				err = domain.Cancel(ctx, q, id, reason)
mc/verbs/task.go:146:		if err := domain.Cancel(ctx, q, task, \"operator_interrupt\"); err != nil {
mc/domain/task.go:421:func Cancel(ctx context.Context, q Q, taskID int64, reason string) error {
mc/domain/task.go:451:	return Cancel(ctx, q, taskID, reason)
mc/domain/initiative_test.go:127:			return domain.Cancel(ctx, q, init, \"descoped\")
mc/domain/initiative_test.go:211:			return domain.Cancel(ctx, q, children[0], \"abandon this line\")
mc/domain/packet_test.go:199:			return domain.Cancel(ctx, q, id, \"drop\")
mc/domain/queue_test.go:39:			return domain.Cancel(ctx, q, a, \"drop\") // cascade archives the packet
mc/domain/queue_test.go:219:			return domain.Cancel(ctx, q, children[0], \"drained for the arc\")
mc/domain/task_test.go:181:			return domain.Cancel(ctx, q, child, \"descoped\")
mc/domain/task_test.go:831:			return domain.Cancel(ctx, q, id, \"direction changed\")
mc/domain/task_test.go:851:			return domain.Cancel(ctx, q, id, \"\")
mc/domain/task_test.go:869:			return domain.Cancel(ctx, q, init, \"descoped\")
mc/domain/task_test.go:901:			return domain.Cancel(ctx, q, init, \"initiative descoped\")"

ADR-020:596 (verbatim) — proof the ADR knows to account for test re-pinning elsewhere, making the omission at 599 an inconsistency:
"  changes; `wave` and `plan_review_sendback` are additive and role-gated, so v1's
  meaning for every existing role is unchanged — but the ADR-008 field list must
  be updated in the same change, and the golden-brief tests re-pinned."

findings.title: D2(b)'s SQL rendering drops the `status = 'seeded'` conjunct its own Go predicate carries
findings.reasoning:
The claim is verified in full against the ADR text, the spec, and the schema.

1. The inconsistency is present in the ADR's text. D2 introduces the predicates with "Two record predicates, defined once and used by both queries:" and defines planReviewPending with an explicit `AND I.status = 'seeded'` conjunct. It then offers a SQL block introduced as "In SQL terms the §10 (3) `NOT EXISTS` arm becomes:" — i.e., the SQL is presented as a rendering of the *same* predicate pair, not as a separate weaker rule. The third disjunct is `EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM tasks c WHERE c.initiative_id = tasks.id AND c.archived = 0 AND c.plan_reviewed = 0)`, with no status test. The `I.scope = 'initiative'` conjunct is legitimately absorbed by the leading `scope = 'task'` disjunct; `I.status = 'seeded'` is not absorbed by anything.

2. Nothing else in query (3) supplies the missing conjunct. Spec §10's query (3) reads `WHERE archived = 0 AND blocked = 0 AND stage_rank > 0 AND (scope = 'task' OR NOT EXISTS (...))`. `stage_rank > 0` excludes only `packaged` (§ spec line 132: "`packaged` ranks 0"), not `worked` or `verified`. So the SQL, as written, would expose an initiative at status `worked`/`verified` with open unreviewed children to (3), where the unchanged (status, scope) table (mc/dispatch/dispatch.go:653-668) maps `StatusWorked → RoleVerifier` / `StatusVerified → RolePackager` — not to the Editor plan review. The Go form (`hasOpenChildren && !planReviewPending`) would park that same row. The two genuinely disagree, and the SQL is the fail-open side.

3. The reviewer's own reachability caveat is also correct, and I confirmed it rather than taking it on trust: `tasks_birth_rules` (schema.sql:155ff) admits a child only "into a live, still-seeded initiative", and `initiatives_declare_requires_drained` (schema.sql:250-256) ABORTs `seeded → worked` while `EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM tasks c WHERE c.initiative_id = OLD.id AND c.archived = 0)`; `tasks_no_unarchive` (schema.sql:229-235) prevents an archived child returning. So an initiative past `seeded` can never have an open child, and the divergence is latent. This bounds the severity to minor — it is a text defect, not a live hole.

4. The "the tests will catch it" defence does not hold. `grep -rn "stage_rank > 0" mc/` returns only dispatch.go:613 (a doc comment) and roles.go:110 (the zero-promotion guard's counting query) — query (3) has no SQL implementation to differentially test. And the suite the ADR points at ("The SQL differential suite gets the new predicate on both queries") is defined by docs/phase2-contract.md §4.4 as a *loader* differential: real-SQL states → `verbs.Dispatch` output equals `dispatch.Decide` over the hand-built projection. Both sides of that comparison are the Go predicate; it structurally cannot detect a Go-vs-SQL divergence in a query that exists only in prose.

5. The ADR holds itself to a higher standard elsewhere, which is why this reads as an oversight rather than a licensed shorthand: D6 ("The wave is uniform in `plan_reviewed`, by construction ... This is not asserted; it follows") supplies exactly the by-construction argument that the omitted status conjunct lacks. D6's argument is about wave uniformity, not about initiative status, so it does not cover this.

Because the SQL block is the normative spec text this ADR proposes to amend into §10, shipping it as written would put a predicate in the spec that is strictly weaker than the design it is supposed to encode, with no stated justification. Fix is one line — either add `AND tasks.status = 'seeded'` to the disjunct, or state that the status test is redundant under strict drain and cite the trigger. Cheap enough to do before TDD, since the SQL is what a builder will translate.

Not a basis for either verdict, and not relied upon: the operator's reading-(i) decision. This finding is about the fidelity of the rendering, not the shape.
findings.evidence:
ADR-020 D2 (docs/adr/020-initiative-wave-plan-review.md:145-155):
"Two record predicates, defined once and used by both queries:"
"planReviewPending(I) :=  I.scope = 'initiative'
                     AND I.status = 'seeded'
                     AND EXISTS (child C : C.initiative_id = I.id
                                        AND C.archived = 0
                                        AND C.plan_reviewed = 0)"

ADR-020 D2(b) (lines 166-175):
"In SQL terms the §10 (3) `NOT EXISTS` arm becomes:
AND (scope = 'task'
     OR NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM tasks c
                    WHERE c.initiative_id = tasks.id AND c.archived = 0)
     OR EXISTS     (SELECT 1 FROM tasks c
                    WHERE c.initiative_id = tasks.id AND c.archived = 0
                      AND c.plan_reviewed = 0))"
— no `status` conjunct.

specs/mission-control-spec.md:378-381 (query (3), the text being amended):
"WHERE archived = 0 AND blocked = 0 AND stage_rank > 0
  AND (scope = 'task' OR NOT EXISTS
       (SELECT 1 FROM tasks c WHERE c.initiative_id = tasks.id AND c.archived = 0))"
— no status test to supply the missing conjunct.

mc/dispatch/dispatch.go:653-668 (spawnFor, the unchanged role map that would receive the over-exposed row):
"	case StatusWorked:
		sp.Role = RoleVerifier // task, or initiative arc (completion report + clean merge)
	case StatusVerified:
		sp.Role = RolePackager // task packet / arc packet"

mc/substrate/schema.sql:248-256 (why the divergence is latent, not live):
"-- Strict drain (§6.1): an initiative may declare done (seeded → worked) only
-- when every child is archived.
CREATE TRIGGER initiatives_declare_requires_drained
BEFORE UPDATE OF status ON tasks
WHEN OLD.scope = 'initiative'
  AND OLD.status = 'seeded' AND NEW.status = 'worked'
  AND EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM tasks c WHERE c.initiative_id = OLD.id AND c.archived = 0)
BEGIN
    SELECT RAISE(ABORT, 'strict drain: initiative has open children (§6.1)');
END;"

mc/substrate/schema.sql:160-164 (tasks_birth_rules — children only into a seeded parent):
"        WHEN NEW.initiative_id IS NOT NULL AND NEW.status <> 'seeded'
            THEN RAISE(ABORT, 'wave children are born seeded (§6.1)')
        WHEN NEW.initiative_id IS NOT NULL AND NOT EXISTS (
                SELECT 1 FROM tasks p
                WHERE p.id = NEW.initiative_id"

docs/phase2-contract.md:252-258 (why the cited suite cannot catch it):
"### 4.4 Loader differential tests (builder B, `mc/verbs/`)
`dispatch_test.go`'s purity never exercises `loadRecords`/`loadLock`/
`applyAction`. Builder B adds a differential suite in
`mc/verbs/dispatchverb_test.go`: states written through real SQL (NULL
columns, derived landing-pending per NOTE(P1.9)/(S6.3), wave children,
briefing activity rows) → `verbs.Dispatch` output equals the effect implied
by `dispatch.Decide` over the hand-built projection."

ADR-020 Tests section (line 571): "The SQL differential suite gets the new predicate on both queries."

ADR-020 D6 (lines 405-408), the by-construction standard the omission does not meet:
"### D6. The wave is uniform in `plan_reviewed`, by construction
Every open child of an initiative always belongs to exactly one wave and always
shares one `plan_reviewed` value. This is not asserted; it follows:"

findings.title: Sharp edge 1's "forever ... a slot that can never free" overstates a stall the operator can always break
findings.reasoning:
Both of the finding's code claims check out verbatim against the primary sources, and the ADR's own text supplies the internal-consistency contrast.

1. "a slot that can never free" is false as written. `domain.Cancel` (mc/domain/task.go:421) writes `archived = 1` on the task row. Sharp edge 1's scenario is an initiative "bounced back from operator review ... packet still holding its slot" — i.e. it has a live packet, so `CancelPacket`'s `requireLivePacket` gate passes and `mc packet cancel` reaches `Cancel`. Archiving the initiative row fires `tasks_archive_cascades_packet` (schema.sql:381, `WHEN OLD.archived = 0 AND NEW.archived = 1` — unscoped, so it catches the initiative's own arc packet via `WHERE task_id = NEW.id`) and `initiatives_archive_cascade` (schema.sql:361), which cancels the open children and archives their packets. The occupancy count in Decide only counts `!p.Archived` packets, so the slot demonstrably frees.

2. "The tick idles `queue-saturated` — forever" is under-specified. `refinementStart` runs at dispatch.go:363, ahead of `return idle(IdleQueueSaturated)` at dispatch.go:374, and it scans `rec.Packets` globally, not just the wedged initiative's. The initiative itself is correctly skipped (`if t.Status != StatusPackaged` at dispatch.go:586 — the ADR's cite is exact), but any *other* unarchived, non-saturated, packaged, undecided, unblocked packet still yields a Refiner spawn. The idle branch's own comment states the precondition the ADR's scenario never establishes: "Every queued packet saturated → idle".

Neither error touches the argument sharp edge 1 is making. The wedge is real (I confirmed the trace end to end: `childGate` hides the children from (2a); `refinementStart` cannot re-enter a `seeded` row; the initiative makes no autonomous progress), and D2's remedy — landing the gate and the exception in (2a) as well as (3) — is correct and necessary. So this is a prose-precision finding, not a design finding.

It is worth fixing rather than ignoring because the ADR itself makes operator-escapability the load-bearing discriminator two edges later: sharp edge 5 downgrades a structurally similar stall precisely because "the operator holds the key, so it is a wait and not a wedge." Under that stated standard, edge 1's stall also has an operator key. The honest distinction — which the ADR should say instead — is that edge 1's only key is *destructive* (cancel annihilates the initiative and its wave) whereas edge 5's is *restorative* (unblock resumes the work untouched). That narrower claim still fully justifies (2a), and it keeps the two edges judged by one standard. As written, a reviewer comparing them will read the standard as having shifted.

Scoring severity: no invariant is at risk, no spec text is contradicted, and no code changes as a result — the ADR is a design doc and the design is right. This is a text tightening in the Consequences section. Hence info, and hence fix-before-TDD rather than park: it is a two-sentence edit with no operator input needed, and the ADR is still Proposed and about to be reviewed, so the cheapest moment to correct it is now, before the sentence is quoted into a test name ("the at-cap refinement-wave case of sharp edge 1 does not idle" is already listed under Tests that pin it, and that test will need the "all other packets saturated" precondition made explicit in its fixture anyway).

findings.title: Sharp edge 1's `dispatch.go:362` cite points at the (2b) comment, not the (2a) spawnFor call
findings.reasoning:
Read probe succeeded (/bin/pwd -P resolved to the repo root; wc -l returned 603 for the ADR and 829 for the spec; no read flapping).

The ADR's Sharp edge 1 states: "the (2a) arm maps by the same (status, scope) table it already uses (dispatch.go:362), so the Editor plan-review arm is reachable at cap." I read mc/dispatch/dispatch.go lines 330-379 directly. Line 362 is the comment "// (2b) Start one on the best non-saturated packet." — i.e. it opens the (2b) arm, not (2a). The (2a) arm spans lines 358-361, and its spawnFor call site is line 360 ("return spawnFor(rec, t)"). The (status, scope) mapping table itself lives inside spawnFor, which the ADR correctly cites elsewhere (line 178) as dispatch.go:653; I verified line 653 is "func spawnFor(rec Records, t Task) Action {".

I independently verified the reviewer's supporting claim that every other dispatch cite in the ADR is exact, via awk on the eight cited line numbers: 515 = "func inFlightRefinement(rec Records) (Task, bool) {", 531 = "if t.Scope == ScopeInitiative && hasOpenChildren(rec, t.ID) {", 586 = "if t.Status != StatusPackaged {", 616 = "func nextDispatch(rec Records) (Task, bool) {", 622 = "if t.Scope == ScopeInitiative && hasOpenChildren(rec, t.ID) {", 634 = "func lessNextDispatch(a, b Task) bool {", 653 = "func spawnFor(rec Records, t Task) Action {", 708 = "func proposedPool(rec Records) []int64 {". Each lands precisely on the named declaration or predicate. Against that uniform precision, the 362 cite is off-by-two drift rather than an intentional block-level pointer.

The finding is cosmetic and does not touch the design. The underlying argument of Sharp edge 1 — that (2a) routes through spawnFor and therefore reaches the plan-review split branch for free, making the Editor arm reachable at cap — is TRUE and is proven by line 360. The at-cap deadlock analysis (refinementStart requiring status = 'packaged' at dispatch.go:586 while a bounced-back initiative sits at 'seeded', hence D2 landing the gate in (2a) as well as (3)) is unaffected. No spec conflict, no invariant at risk. This does not touch the operator's settled reading-(i) decision.

Disposition fix-adr-before-tdd: the ADR's line-number cites are load-bearing for the implementer who will read them against the file during TDD, and the correction (362 -> 360, or 359-360) is a one-token edit. Cheap to fix now, mildly misleading if left.
findings.evidence:
ADR docs/adr/020-initiative-wave-plan-review.md:480-484 — "D2 therefore lands both the gate and\n   the exception in (2a) as well as (3); the (2a) arm maps by the same\n   (status, scope) table it already uses (dispatch.go:362), so the Editor\n   plan-review arm is reachable at cap."

mc/dispatch/dispatch.go:356-363 (verbatim, via Read) —

Line 362 is the (2b) comment; the (2a) spawnFor call is line 360.

Control cites verified exact (awk on mc/dispatch/dispatch.go) —
"515: func inFlightRefinement(rec Records) (Task, bool) {"
"531: \t\tif t.Scope == ScopeInitiative && hasOpenChildren(rec, t.ID) {"

"622: \t\tif t.Scope == ScopeInitiative && hasOpenChildren(rec, t.ID) {"
"634: func lessNextDispatch(a, b Task) bool {"

findings.title: D1 claims children_work_requires_plan_review prevents *committed* child work; a SQLite trigger structurally cannot, and the residual failure mode is unaddressed
findings.reasoning:
The core claim is correct and backed by decisive text in three independent sources.

1. THE ADR DOES MAKE THE CLAIM. D1 (docs/adr/020-initiative-wave-plan-review.md:122-126) justifies `children_work_requires_plan_review` on the ground that it prevents *committed* work, and the Consequences headline (:437-440) asserts the guarantee holds "twice over (the dispatch filter, and `children_work_requires_plan_review` in storage)". Both quotes are verbatim-accurate as offered.

2. A SQLITE TRIGGER STRUCTURALLY CANNOT REACH THE COMMIT. The git commit is not a spine write and does not happen inside the transaction the trigger guards:
   - spec §6.2 (line 200) opens with "This is contract enforced by `mc`'s runtime and the role briefs, not by SQLite."
   - spec §6.2 further pins where the commit executes: "Every mutating git operation Mission Control performs on a Worksource — worktree add/remove, branch, commit, merge, including the landing (§7) — runs only inside a container at the fixed canonical mount path".
   - spec §10 "Completion is a pure write" (line 402): "The completing agent itself invokes `mc complete` ... `mc complete` writes the role's report, advances the subject's status in the same transaction (Inv. 10), releases the lease, and returns".
   I confirmed this against the real code rather than assuming: `mc/verbs/complete.go` performs no git operation anywhere in its body. It fences the run, reads `scope`/`initiative_id`, validates `--branch` as a *recorded string* ("Worker: records tasks.branch"), and calls into domain. The commit is therefore already on the initiative's shared branch, made by the Worker agent in-container, before `mc complete` is ever invoked.

3. THE CITE THE FINDING RESTS ON IS ACCURATE. mc/domain/task.go:109-111 is exactly `UPDATE tasks SET status = ? WHERE id = ?` inside `AdvanceStage` (declared at task.go:74). A `BEFORE UPDATE OF status` trigger aborts that write and nothing else. The schema header's own scope statement agrees: "a bug in mc cannot write an illegal state because the storage rejects it" — *write*, i.e. stored state, not filesystem/git effects.

So on D1's own stated premise (a bug in the D2 dispatch filter), the trigger yields: unreviewed changes committed to the shared branch, `mc complete` aborting, and the run exiting without an accepted terminal — recovered by the lease rules, charging `dispatch_retries`. The trigger keeps the illegal *state* out of storage (which is genuinely §4's job and genuinely worth having), but it does not and cannot deliver the anti-commit guarantee D1 claims for it.

WHAT I TRIMMED FROM THE FINDING (two overreaches, neither fatal to the verdict):

(a) SEVERITY — downgraded major → minor. The finding does not identify any defect in the *design*. Every mechanism ADR-020 specifies is correct as written: the trigger is correct, the D2 filter is correct, and the ADR's Inv. 25 bullet (:455-458) reasons correctly *because* it invokes dispatchability rather than the trigger — the reviewer concedes this. The defect is confined to two prose passages that overstate a safety guarantee. Nothing that gets implemented changes. That is a documentation-accuracy defect, not an invariant break or an unimplementable spec. It is still worth fixing before TDD precisely because an overstated safety guarantee in a headline Consequences bullet is the kind of claim a future reader relies on.

(b) "NO REVERT PATH" — not supported. The reviewer asserts the stranded state has "no revert path" and is "a state the ADR never analyses". The spec already supplies the path at §6.2: "a rare in-flight cancellation is one `git revert`", and §10's crash table plus Inv. 25's "An interrupted or crashed run leaves nothing in the path of subsequent work — its partial output sits uncommitted and inert, removable host-side with no agent involvement" cover the recovery story generally. A wrongly-committed child under a hypothetical `mc dispatch` bug is a pre-existing spec-level hazard, not one ADR-020 introduces or owes an analysis of. I confirm the finding on the overstatement, not on this residual-failure-mode framing.

FIX: two localized text edits — D1's rationale sentence should claim what the trigger actually does (keep the illegal *state* — an unreviewed child at `worked` — out of storage; the dispatch filter is what prevents the commit, because the commit is an in-container runtime effect §6.2 explicitly places outside SQLite's reach), and the Consequences bullet should drop "twice over" or restate the two guards as covering different things (filter → dispatch and therefore the commit; trigger → stored state).

Note: the operator's reading-(i) decision played no part in this verdict — the finding does not touch the gate's shape, only the ADR's description of one guard's reach.
findings.evidence:
THE ADR'S CLAIM (docs/adr/020-initiative-wave-plan-review.md:122-126, D1):
"This is the redundant backstop doing exactly the job §4 assigns it: a bug in `mc dispatch` cannot produce *committed child work* (Inv. 25 — a child's changes commit in the same breath as its `seeded → worked`) that the Editor never approved. The dispatch-side filter (D2) is the policy; this is the fence."

THE ADR'S CLAIM (:437-440, Consequences):
"**Inv. 12 becomes true for waves.** Today a wave child can be Worked and committed before any judge reads its criteria; after this, it cannot — twice over (the dispatch filter, and `children_work_requires_plan_review` in storage)."

DECISIVE — specs/mission-control-spec.md §6.2 (line 200), opening sentence:
"This is contract enforced by `mc`'s runtime and the role briefs, not by SQLite."

DECISIVE — specs/mission-control-spec.md §6.2 (line 202), where the commit executes:
"**Mission Control's git runs in-container, at the canonical mount path.** Every mutating git operation Mission Control performs on a Worksource — worktree add/remove, branch, commit, merge, including the landing (§7) — runs only inside a container at the fixed canonical mount path; Mission Control never mutates a Worksource from the host."

DECISIVE — specs/mission-control-spec.md §10 "Completion is a pure write" (line 402):
"The completing agent itself invokes `mc complete` through its role-scoped capability — there is no report file and no replay ... `mc complete` writes the role's report, advances the subject's status in the same transaction (Inv. 10), releases the lease, and returns — **it never dispatches**."

THE ONLY SPINE EFFECT THE TRIGGER CAN REACH — mc/domain/task.go:109-111 (cite verified accurate):
"	if _, err := q.ExecContext(ctx,
		`UPDATE tasks SET status = ? WHERE id = ?`, to, taskID); err != nil {
		return err"

SCOPE OF THE TRIGGER LAYER — mc/substrate/schema.sql:7-10:
"-- This file is the redundant backstop layer (spec §4 \"make impossible states
-- impossible\"): a bug in mc cannot write an illegal state because the storage
-- rejects it. Real domain enforcement lives above, in the Phase 2 layer;
-- every rule here must hold standalone."

CODE CONFIRMATION (mc/verbs/complete.go:12-15) — --branch is a recorded string, not a git action; Complete's body contains no git operation:
"type CompleteArgs struct {
	Task    int64
	Run     string
	Status  string // worked | packaged | seeded (\"\" for the flag terminals)
	Branch  string // Worker: records tasks.branch (phase1b Ambiguity A2)"

THE ADR GETS IT RIGHT ELSEWHERE (:455-456) — correct because it invokes dispatchability, not the trigger:
"**Inv. 25 holds trivially** on the send-back path: an unreviewed child was never dispatchable, so it has no worktree changes and no commit to revert."

AGAINST THE REVIEWER'S \"no revert path\" — specs/mission-control-spec.md §6.2 (line 200):
"A child's changes are committed, tagged with its task id, in the same breath as its Worker-stage completion: the Verifier verifies a commit, the packet cites a stable range, and a rare in-flight cancellation is one `git revert`."

findings.title: D1's fourth bullet directs the §4 enforcement row into a "rule list in the schema header" that does not exist
findings.reasoning:
Every load-bearing element of the claim survives independent verification against the primary sources.

1. The ADR bullet says what the finding quotes. docs/adr/020-initiative-wave-plan-review.md:127-129 reads verbatim: "- **§4 table row** (add to the spec-mirroring rule list in the schema header): \"Wave children work only after the Editor's plan review | `children_work_requires_plan_review` trigger\"." The row is pipe-delimited — the format of a markdown table cell, not of a SQL comment.

2. The named target does not exist. mc/substrate/schema.sql:1-18 is the entire header (line 20 opens the `meta` section banner). It is continuous prose: the behavioral-contract pointer, the backstop-role paragraph, and the connection-discipline paragraph. `grep -c '|' mc/substrate/schema.sql` returns 2, and `grep -n '|'` locates both at lines 311 and 320 — `'blocked child #' || NEW.id`, SQL string concatenation inside the propagation triggers. There is no table, no rule list, and no pipe-delimited anything in the header or anywhere else in the file. I also grepped the whole repo for "spec-mirroring": the only hit is the ADR's own bullet. The ADR names a structure that does not exist and that no other document has ever named.

3. The row's real home is spec text. specs/mission-control-spec.md:79-89 carries the actual §4 table under "**Make-impossible-states-impossible at the substrate.**", with the exact `| Rule | Enforcement |` header and rows in precisely the format the ADR's bullet writes (e.g. "| Wave children born `seeded`, into a live seeded initiative only | `children_birth_rules` trigger |"). The ADR's proposed row is a §4 spec row wearing a schema-header address.

4. The ADR is internally inconsistent about which of the two it means. Line 107-108 introduces the bullet as "Three substrate rules, in `mc/substrate/schema.sql`, and a fourth row for **§4's enforcement table**" — the spec. Line 127 then relocates it to "the schema header" — the code file. The two sentences name different files, and only one of them contains a table. So the bullet is unimplementable as written under its own text, and its only actionable reading (edit specs/mission-control-spec.md:81-97) is an edit to the document AGENTS.md calls the behavioral contract, which "wins on conflict" and whose gaps are supposed to route through IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES.md per AGENTS.md §6. The ADR nowhere flags this bullet as a spec edit; it is presented as one of four routine substrate deliverables.

5. Nothing pins it. I read the full "Tests that pin it" section (lines 554-589). Its Substrate bullet enumerates only executable obligations — born `plan_reviewed = 0`, the birth abort, the non-child CHECK, one-way, and `children_work_requires_plan_review` aborting/permitting `seeded → worked`. No test, checklist item, or "end of the line" gate covers the fourth bullet's row. The three code deliverables are all test-pinned; the fourth is a documentation obligation with no enforcement, so it can silently never land and TDD would still go green.

Scope check: this finding turns entirely on the ADR's text versus the real schema file and the real spec table. It does not touch, depend on, or reopen the operator's settled reading-(i) decision — the review gate itself, the trigger's semantics, and the D1 carrier design are all untouched by this and are, as far as this lens goes, correctly specified.

Severity is minor, not major, and I want to be precise about why rather than inflate it. The gate this row would document is itself fully and correctly specified at lines 119-126 and pinned by two substrate tests. No invariant is breached, no state machine is wrong, and no code would be built incorrectly from this ADR. What is broken is a deliverable's address: one of four bullets points at a place that does not exist, and the only real place it could point is a file the standing instruction treats as a different class of artifact. The cost is a deliverable that quietly evaporates plus a latent unflagged spec edit — real, cheap to fix, not dangerous.
findings.evidence:
ADR-020:107-108 (the intro, naming the spec's table): "Three substrate rules, in `mc/substrate/schema.sql`, and a fourth row for §4's enforcement table:"

ADR-020:127-129 (the bullet, naming the schema header instead): "- **§4 table row** (add to the spec-mirroring rule list in the schema header): "Wave children work only after the Editor's plan review | `children_work_requires_plan_review` trigger"."

mc/substrate/schema.sql:1-18 — the complete header, prose only, no table: "-- Mission Control spine schema — Phase 1a substrate.
--
-- Behavioral contract: specs/mission-control-spec.md (§4 canonical state,
-- §5 core entities, §6/§6.1 state machine, §7 decisions, §8 WIP cap and
-- saturation, §10 lock/runs, §15.4/§15.5 Homie state and outbox, §16.4 meta).
--
-- This file is the redundant backstop layer (spec §4 "make impossible states
-- impossible"): a bug in mc cannot write an illegal state because the storage
-- rejects it. Real domain enforcement lives above, in the Phase 2 layer;
-- every rule here must hold standalone.
--
-- Connection discipline (spec §10, validated by spike S5): the schema is
-- WAL-compatible and every connection must open with
--   _pragma=busy_timeout(N) & _pragma=journal_mode(WAL) & _pragma=foreign_keys(1)
-- and run every mutation under BEGIN IMMEDIATE. The trigger lattice below is
-- deliberately recursive_triggers-agnostic: no trigger depends on another
-- trigger firing from inside its body — every cascade writes all of its
-- downstream effects directly (NOTE(P1.12) in NOTES.md)."

`grep -n '|' mc/substrate/schema.sql` — the file's only two pipes, both SQL concatenation, neither a table:
"311:    SET blocked = 1, blocked_reason = 'blocked child #' || NEW.id"
"320:    SET blocked = 1, blocked_reason = 'blocked child #' || NEW.id"

specs/mission-control-spec.md:79-89 — the real §4 table, in the exact format the ADR's row is written in: "**Make-impossible-states-impossible at the substrate.** A bug in `mc` cannot write an illegal state, because the storage rejects it:

| Rule | Enforcement |
|---|---|
| Only legal status transitions (the §6 machine, both scopes) | `BEFORE UPDATE` trigger vs. the legal-transition list |
... | Wave children born `seeded`, into a live seeded initiative only | `children_birth_rules` trigger |"

ADR-020:558-562 — the Substrate test obligations, covering the three triggers and nothing else: "- **Substrate** (`mc/substrate/substrate_test.go`): children born `plan_reviewed = 0`; the birth abort on `plan_reviewed = 1`; the non-child `CHECK`; one-way (1 → 0 aborts; 0 → 1 on an archived, decided, or non-child row aborts); `children_work_requires_plan_review` aborting a `seeded → worked` on an unreviewed child *and* permitting it at 1."

AGENTS.md (header): "The behavioral contract is `specs/mission-control-spec.md`; the operating protocol is `specs/implementation-handoff.md`. **The spec wins on conflict.**"

AGENTS.md §6: "Deviations from the spec land in `IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES.md` (append-only, newest last, entry template at the top of that file)."

Negative result (repo-wide grep for "spec-mirroring"): the only occurrence in the entire repository is ADR-020:127 itself — no schema file, spec, or other ADR uses the term.

findings.title: D4's "nothing else ... holds here for free" is contradicted by brief.go: LatestOutputPath is loaded unconditionally for every subject role, and for an initiative it is Strategist(initiative)'s own completion report
findings.reasoning:
I verified the finding's full causal chain against the primary sources; every link holds.

1. THE CODE CITE IS ACCURATE. mc/verbs/brief.go:68-85 loads LatestOutputPath for ANY spawn with a non-nil SubjectID. The block sits at lines 74-84, entirely OUTSIDE the `if sp.Role == dispatch.RoleEditor` gate that begins at line 87. Every other enrichment in buildSpawnBrief is role-gated (RoleEditor:87, RoleStrategistPropose:96, RoleWorker:99, RolePackager:106, RoleStrategistConsole:113) — LatestOutputPath is the sole exception. The ADR itself notices the RoleEditor gate exists ("exactly as `proposed_pool` is rendered only for `RoleEditor` (brief.go:87)") while asserting "No change" for the subject path directly above it.

2. THE PLAN-REVIEW SPAWN HAS SubjectID SET. The ADR's own D4 requires it ("the lease subject is the initiative"), and D5's fence 3 requires "the returned subject must equal `--initiative`". So the LatestOutputPath query fires on every plan review.

3. THE WRITE SIDE IS REACHABLE AND MANDATORY, NOT INCIDENTAL. mc/verbs/complete.go:110-115 does not merely permit an initiative done-declaration with --outputs — it REQUIRES it: `if scope == "initiative" { if id.Role != "strategist(initiative)" { return roleMismatch(...) } if a.Outputs == "" { return Usagef("initiative done-declaration requires --outputs <completion report> (ADR-001 D4)") } }`. complete.go:203-206 then writes it: `UPDATE runs SET output_path = ? WHERE id = ? AND output_path IS NULL`. domain.Claim (mc/domain/lease.go:89-93) inserts that run with `subject` = the SubjectID, so the run row the brief's query selects — `WHERE subject = ?` — is exactly the Strategist(initiative)'s own done-declaration run. There is no path by which an initiative reaches `worked` WITHOUT depositing a producer-authored report on a run whose subject is the initiative.

4. THE PATH BACK TO A PLAN REVIEW IS ORDINARY, NOT EXOTIC. spec §6.1:196 states both round-trips verbatim; both return the initiative to `seeded`, where drain re-fires, Strategist(initiative) births a wave, and (under this ADR) planReviewPending makes the Editor plan review the dispatch. The brief for that Editor run then carries latest_output_path = the producer's own completion report.

5. WHY THE ADR'S ARGUMENT IS UNSOUND, NOT JUST INCOMPLETE. The load-bearing word is "records". D4 reasons: the brief is built from records only, therefore the Strategist's reasoning is excluded "for free". That inference does not hold. `output_path` IS a spine record, but its REFERENT is producer-authored prose — the criterion-to-evidence completion report. Records-only buys blindness to a producer's *session*, not to a producer's *authored artifacts*, and this column is precisely a pointer to the latter. ADR-008's own field list confirms the artifact's nature and its unconditional attachment: "every subject role: ... and the latest `mc complete --outputs` report/artifact reference". So the very ADR the design is written against documents this as an every-subject-role field — the ADR-020 author read ADR-008's list and still concluded the exclusion was free.

6. CONSEQUENCE. Two ADR-020 claims are false as written: D4's "nothing else ... holds here for free", and the Consequences' Inv. 9 argument "blind to the producer's reasoning because the brief is records-only" — which is the ADR's central justification that Inv. 9 "extends to wave planning with no new mechanism". The ADR's own pinned test, "the plan-review brief carries the charter and the full wave and nothing else", cannot pass against the design as specified. Red-first TDD would surface it, but the ADR instructs the implementer that no change is needed here, so the natural resolution would be to weaken the test rather than add the gate.

SCOPE NOTE (in the finding's favour, and correctly stated by it): on a virgin initiative's FIRST plan review no prior initiative-subject run carries output_path, so latest_output_path is empty and the property holds by accident. The finding does not overclaim — it scopes the leak to "every plan review after an arc round-trip". A property that holds only until the first round-trip is not a property.

This is not a manufactured finding: it is a one-line divergence between the ADR's text and the code it cites by line number, with a decisive quote on both sides. The fix is small (gate the LatestOutputPath block, or exclude it for RoleEditorPlanReview), but D4 must SPECIFY it rather than assert the property is free — which is exactly what the finding says. It does not touch the operator's settled reading-(i) decision; it is about whether this ADR safely specifies that shape.

SEVERITY: major, not minor. It is not merely a missing line — it falsifies the ADR's stated Inv. 9 argument, which is one of the two invariant claims ("What this buys") the design rests on. An implementer who trusts D4's "No change" ships a plan-review Editor that reads the producer's own report, defeating the decorrelation the gate exists to provide.

findings.title: D5's precedent for a non-operator `decision='cancelled'` is misstated: `initiatives_archive_cascade` never fires without an operator in the loop
findings.reasoning:
The finding is correct on the decisive point and I confirmed it by exhaustive enumeration rather than by accepting the reviewer's trace.

1. The ADR text is as quoted (020:389-394). The cascade trigger text and its WHEN clause are as quoted (schema.sql:361-369). Both cites are accurate.

2. The cascade fires only on an initiative crossing archived 0->1. I enumerated every writer of `archived = 1` on tasks in the whole tree via grep: RejectProposal (domain/task.go:59), Approve (domain/task.go:409), Cancel (domain/task.go:433), and verbs/land.go:52. There are no others.

3. For each, I asked whether it can archive an initiative that still has open children — the only case where the cascade writes anything at all:
   - Approve (and the land.go:52 post-approval archive) require status='packaged', reachable only through 'worked', which for scope='initiative' is strict-drain-guarded in AdvanceStage (task.go:361-367: refuses while any child has archived = 0). So at archive time there are zero open children and the cascade body updates zero rows.
   - Cancel is operator-gated at both call sites: TaskInterrupt calls RequireOperatorVerb(id, "task.interrupt") (verbs/task.go:127) and requireOperatorVerbTx (verbs/task.go:132) before reaching domain.Cancel (verbs/task.go:146); PacketDecide calls requireOperatorVerbTx(ctx, q, id, "packet.decide") (verbs/packet.go:42) before CancelPacket (verbs/packet.go:60). §18 deny rule 1 (spec:820) makes these host-scope for pipeline containers.
   - RejectProposal is the strongest counter-argument available and the reviewer did not raise it: it is explicitly the EDITOR's arm ("RejectProposal is the Editor's reject arm (ADR-001 D4)", task.go:35) and it does write archived = 1 — a genuine non-operator archiver. If it could archive an initiative with open children, the ADR's claim would stand and the finding would be REFUTED. It cannot: it refuses any row whose status is not 'proposed' (task.go:54-56), and the birth-rules trigger (schema.sql:161-166) permits children only into an initiative that is scope='initiative' AND status='seeded' AND archived=0. A 'proposed' initiative therefore has no children by construction, so the Editor's reject fires the cascade as a no-op.

Conclusion: every firing of initiatives_archive_cascade that actually writes decision='cancelled' onto a child is rooted in domain.Cancel, i.e. in an operator decision. The ADR's "with no operator in the loop" is false as a characterisation, and the cascade is not a "standing proof" that a non-operator writer of the mark already exists — it is mechanical propagation of an operator's decision.

Tempering, in fairness to the ADR: the adjacent narrower claim survives and should not be swept away in the fix. §6's gloss genuinely is not an exclusive-writer clause, and the literal SQL writer of the children's marks is the trigger body, not domain.Cancel. The alternative-considered reasoning is also sound (RejectProposal is 'proposed'-only; §6.1's own word is "cancelled"). The defect is confined to the overclaim and its use as proof — it is a rationale defect, not a design defect, and the operator's settled reading-(i) shape is untouched by it.

Severity minor: no invariant is broken, no code exists yet, and the send-back arm is defensible on its own merits. It is not merely info because the false proof sits at a §18-deny-rule-1-adjacent boundary, where it tells a reviewer or the operator that nothing new is happening when in fact ADR-020 introduces the system's first agent-originated cancellation against §6's text. That is exactly the kind of assurance that must be earned, not asserted.

Disposition fix-adr-before-tdd: the fix is a paragraph rewrite in the ADR's rationale with zero design change — own the novelty (state that this is the first agent-originated 'cancelled' write, keep the honest actor='editor' activity row, and keep the surviving narrow point that §6's gloss names the operator's verb rather than an exclusive writer) instead of denying it. Cheap now, and leaving it would carry a false premise into the tests that pin this behaviour.
findings.evidence:
ADR-020 (docs/adr/020-initiative-wave-plan-review.md:389-394), the claim under review:
"`decision = 'cancelled'` written by something other than the operator is not a novelty this ADR introduces: `initiatives_archive_cascade` (schema.sql:365) already writes exactly that mark, mechanically, with no operator in the loop. §6's gloss (\"`cancelled` by the operator at any stage\") describes the operator's verb, not an exclusive writer, and the substrate's own cascade is the standing proof."

mc/substrate/schema.sql:361-369 — the cascade fires only downstream of an initiative archive:
"CREATE TRIGGER initiatives_archive_cascade
AFTER UPDATE OF archived ON tasks
WHEN NEW.scope = 'initiative' AND OLD.archived = 0 AND NEW.archived = 1
BEGIN
    UPDATE tasks
    SET decision   = 'cancelled',
        decided_at = datetime('now'),
        archived   = 1
    WHERE initiative_id = NEW.id AND archived = 0;"

mc/domain/task.go:417-420 — the sole path to that archive-with-open-children:
"// Cancel is the operator cancel: decision='cancelled' + archive, reason
// mandatory (§7). For an initiative the substrate cascade cancels open
// children and archives their packets (§6.1) — one implementation, in the
// lattice."

mc/verbs/task.go:127 and :146 — Cancel's first call site is operator-gated:
"	if err := RequireOperatorVerb(id, \"task.interrupt\"); err != nil {"
"		if err := domain.Cancel(ctx, q, task, \"operator_interrupt\"); err != nil {"

mc/verbs/packet.go:42 and :60 — Cancel's second call site is operator-gated:
"		if err := requireOperatorVerbTx(ctx, q, id, \"packet.decide\"); err != nil {"
"			if err := domain.CancelPacket(ctx, q, task, reason); err != nil {"

DECISIVE, and not cited by the original reviewer — the one non-operator archiver (the Editor) provably cannot reach the cascade with effect.
mc/domain/task.go:35-36 and :54-56 — RejectProposal is the Editor's, and is 'proposed'-only:
"// RejectProposal is the Editor's reject arm (ADR-001 D4): decision='rejected'
// + archive, reason mandatory."
"	if r.Status != \"proposed\" {
		return Errf(CodeIllegalTransition,
			\"only proposed rows are rejected (§6); task %d is %q\", taskID, r.Status)"

mc/substrate/schema.sql:161-166 — children exist only under a SEEDED initiative, so a 'proposed' initiative has none:
"        WHEN NEW.initiative_id IS NOT NULL AND NOT EXISTS (
                SELECT 1 FROM tasks p
                WHERE p.id = NEW.initiative_id
                  AND p.scope = 'initiative'
                  AND p.status = 'seeded'
                  AND p.archived = 0)
            THEN RAISE(ABORT, 'wave children are born only into a live, still-seeded initiative (§6.1)')"

mc/domain/task.go:365-367 — and the Approve/land archive path is strict-drain-guarded to zero open children, making the cascade a no-op there:
"			if open > 0 {
				return Errf(CodeStrictDrain,
					\"strict drain: initiative %d has %d open children (§6.1)\", taskID, open)"

spec §6 (specs/mission-control-spec.md:173):
"- **Terminal bookkeeping is `decision` + `archived`**, orthogonal to status: `approved` only from `packaged` (substrate-enforced), `rejected` at `proposed` by the Editor, `cancelled` by the operator at any stage."

spec §18 (specs/mission-control-spec.md:820):
"1. **No pipeline agent gets operator verbs.** `packet decide`, `task add`/`initiative add` (they file `origin:user` — operator provenance), `task unblock`, `reset`, and `onboard` are host-scope for pipeline containers."

findings.title: D4's `plan_review_sendback` has no recency scoping: "the latest wave.sent_back for this initiative" rides every future wave boundary forever
findings.reasoning: The ADR's D4 field definition is textually unconditional and no other passage in the ADR bounds it. Grepped every send-back mention (lines 116, 239, 274-276, 282, 291, 334, 338, 365, 379, 401, 414, 448, 455, 486-499, 575-582, 594): none scopes the field to a round, wave, or superseding event; line 582 re-pins the unscoped form as a test obligation. Append-only is confirmed at the exact cited lines (schema.sql:693/699), so the row never disappears, and D5's pass arm (line 353) writes kind 'wave.passed', which the field never reads — nothing masks a prior objection. Multi-wave reachability is confirmed by spec:190 (lazy decomposition "at each boundary") + spec:192 (strict drain), so an initiative has many wave boundaries; ADR line 383 dispatches Strategist(initiative) at each drained boundary, and its brief would re-serve the answered objection every time. Internal inconsistency is decisive: the rationale at 276-277 says the Editor "just refused" and D3:239 calls the reason "the objection Strategist(initiative) must answer" — a current-round semantics the definition does not deliver. One caveat on the finding's reasoning (not its verdict): its NOTE(P2.2) disanalogy is overstated, since a §8 refinement round-trip (packaged → seeded) re-seeds a task that already has a correct-verdict run, so the precedent plausibly shares the same exposure; but that cuts against the ADR's use of the precedent as a safety argument rather than rescuing it — the precedent licenses the storage choice (activity row vs. column), never recency semantics, which the ADR never states. Severity minor: no invariant breaks, no state corruption, no slot leak, and the field carries a timestamp; the cost is a degraded LLM brief input. The Inv. 20 framing in the finding is decorative, not load-bearing. Disposition is fix-before-TDD rather than log-as-known specifically because line 582 would freeze the gap into a green test, and the repair is cheap additive prose.
findings.evidence: ADR-020 D4 (lines 274-277): "The Strategist(initiative) brief gains one field, `plan_review_sendback`: the **latest** `wave.sent_back` activity row for this initiative (prose + timestamp), rendered only for `RoleStrategistInitiative`. Without it the send-back is a silent loop — the Strategist replans blind and re-pitches the wave the Editor just refused." | ADR-020 D5 pass arm (line 352-354): "`INSERT INTO activity (actor, kind, subject, detail) VALUES ('editor', 'wave.passed', <initiative id>, <child ids>)`" | ADR-020 D3 (line 239): "a send-back's reason is the objection Strategist(initiative) must answer" | ADR-020 tests (line 581-582): "the Strategist(initiative) brief carries the latest send-back and does not clobber `refine_notes`" | mc/substrate/schema.sql:693-697: "CREATE TRIGGER activity_append_only_update / BEFORE UPDATE ON activity / BEGIN / SELECT RAISE(ABORT, 'activity is append-only (Inv. 7)'); / END;" | mc/substrate/schema.sql:699-703: "CREATE TRIGGER activity_append_only_delete / BEFORE DELETE ON activity" | spec §6.1 (line 190): "**Waves.** Strategist(initiative) decomposes lazily: at each boundary it instantiates only the tasks that are actionable *right now*." | spec §6.1 (line 192): "**Strict drain.** The next wave (or the done-declaration) is owed only when *every* child is operator-decided and archived." | spec Inv. 7 (line 24): "**Activity log is append-only.** A substrate trigger forbids `UPDATE`/`DELETE`." | ADR-020 D5 (line 383-385): "The initiative now has zero open children ⇒ **drained** ⇒ the next tick's query (3) selects it (`seeded`, initiative, `planReviewPending` false) and dispatches Strategist(initiative), whose brief carries the objection." | docs/phase2-contract.md:277-278: "the next Worker brief's correction file is *queried* from the subject's latest correct-verdict run, no task column needed."

findings.title: ADR-020 keeps `mc.spawn-brief.v1` while quoting only the first half of ADR-008's rule, whose stated remedy is "add a new version"
findings.reasoning:
The finding's quotes are all verbatim accurate, and its narrow factual observation is true: ADR-020 paraphrases ADR-008 as "brief changes are schema changes" without the words "add a new version", and it keeps v1. But every consequential claim built on that observation is falsified by the primary sources.

(1) "reached by partial quotation rather than by an explicit, argued amendment" is refuted by ADR-020's own sentence, which contains the argument AND the reconciliation action in the same breath as the paraphrase: "additive and role-gated, so v1's meaning for every existing role is unchanged — but the ADR-008 field list must be updated in the same change, and the golden-brief tests re-pinned." That is an explicit, argued, in-place amendment proposal — the same pattern this repo already sanctions in ADR-017 ("This ADR stays Accepted; this amendment corrects it in place rather than superseding it"). It is not a gloss.

(2) The argument is substantiated by the actual code. Every role-gated field in spawnBriefDocument (mc/verbs/brief.go:39-52) is `omitempty` — ProposedPool, DedupeTitles, ReviewQueue, BlockedTasks. An additive role-gated field therefore leaves every non-populating role's rendered v1 document byte-identical. ADR-008's prohibition is on "silently changing the meaning of mc.spawn-brief.v1"; neither the meaning nor the bytes change for existing roles, and the change is not silent (ADR + updated field list + tests).

(3) The AGENTS.md §6 remedy is misrouted. §6 verbatim: "Deviations from the spec land in IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES.md ... *Planned* designs the spec delegates go in docs/adr/ instead." ADR-008 is not the spec, and ADR-020 is a planned design sitting in docs/adr/ — exactly where §6 directs it. §6 does not govern ADR-to-ADR refinement.

(4) The claimed "compounding" with D4's pool_snapshot reinterpretation is not a rule violation: runs.pool_snapshot is a schema column, never mentioned anywhere in ADR-008, whose only frozen carrier is the brief document. "A frozen carrier's neighbourhood" is not a rule in any source.

(5) The finding's implied reading of ADR-008 ("any field addition ⇒ new version") contradicts ADR-001:178-179, which licenses role addition as "new verbs + table row, no scope-mechanism change". Under that reading every new role would force a brief version bump. The reading ADR-020 takes — that the prohibited act is silently changing v1's meaning — harmonizes the two accepted ADRs; the finding's reading sets them against each other.

Note (outside this finding's scope, flagged for a different lens): ADR-020 says "the golden-brief tests re-pinned", but `grep -rn "buildSpawnBrief|spawnBriefSchema|spawn-brief" mc/ --include=*_test.go` returns nothing — no golden-brief tests exist to re-pin.
findings.evidence:
ADR-020:593-596 (the full bullet the finding says omits the argument): "- **`mc.spawn-brief.v1` gains fields.** ADR-008 says brief changes are schema changes; `wave` and `plan_review_sendback` are additive and role-gated, so v1's meaning for every existing role is unchanged — but the ADR-008 field list must be updated in the same change, and the golden-brief tests re-pinned."

ADR-008:38-39 (the rule; the operative prohibition is the "silently changing the meaning" clause): "- Brief changes are schema changes: add a new version and tests rather than silently changing the meaning of `mc.spawn-brief.v1`."

mc/verbs/brief.go:39-52 (every role-gated field is omitempty ⇒ additive role-gated fields leave existing roles' documents byte-identical): "type spawnBriefDocument struct {
	Schema           string        `json:"schema"`
	Role             string        `json:"role"`
	Directive        string        `json:"directive"`
	Subject          *briefTask    `json:"subject,omitempty"`
	ProposedPool     []briefTask   `json:"proposed_pool,omitempty"`
	DedupeTitles     []string      `json:"dedupe_titles,omitempty"`
	LatestCorrection *briefVerdict `json:"latest_correction,omitempty"`
	LatestVerdict    *briefVerdict `json:"latest_verdict,omitempty"`
	LatestOutputPath string        `json:"latest_output_path,omitempty"`
	ReviewQueue      []briefTask   `json:"review_queue,omitempty"`
	BlockedTasks     []briefTask   `json:"blocked_tasks,omitempty"`
}"

AGENTS.md §6 (refutes the "per AGENTS.md §6 this needs an IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES deviation entry" remedy — ADR-008 is not the spec, and docs/adr/ is where §6 sends planned designs): "Deviations from the spec land in `IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES.md` (append-only, newest last, entry template at the top of that file). *Planned* designs the spec delegates go in `docs/adr/` instead."

docs/adr/001-role-side-verbs.md:178-179 (refutes the "any field addition ⇒ new version" reading, which would make every role addition a version bump): "- `run.json`-derived role matching means adding a role someday = new verbs
  + table row, no scope-mechanism change."

docs/adr/017-mount-authorization.md:10-13 (the repo's established in-place-amendment precedent, which ADR-020's "the ADR-008 field list must be updated in the same change" follows): "## Amendment — 2026-07-14 (view discipline and the privileged-tree gate)

This ADR stays Accepted; this amendment corrects it in place rather than
superseding it, and quotes what it replaces so the correction is visible."

findings.title: D1 overstates what `children_work_requires_plan_review` can fence: it blocks the record of child work, not the git commit
findings.reasoning:
The claim holds on the primary sources, and the ordering that makes it true is pinned by a source the reviewer did not cite: the frozen Worker directive.

1. What the trigger is. ADR-020 D1 specifies `children_work_requires_plan_review` as `BEFORE UPDATE OF status WHEN OLD.initiative_id IS NOT NULL AND OLD.plan_reviewed = 0 AND OLD.status = 'seeded' AND NEW.status = 'worked'` → ABORT. Its entire reach is the `tasks.status` write inside the SQLite transaction.

2. The commit is not in that transaction, and the spec says so in terms. §6.2 opens: "This is contract enforced by `mc`'s runtime and the role briefs, not by SQLite." The git operations it governs ("Every mutating git operation Mission Control performs on a Worksource — worktree add/remove, branch, commit, merge") run "only inside a container at the fixed canonical mount path". No trigger can reach them.

3. The ordering is commit-then-record, so the abort lands too late. `mc/verbs/directives/worker.md` — an authored, frozen role directive, i.e. exactly one of the "role briefs" §6.2 names as the enforcement locus — instructs: "For mutable work, finish with one committed branch state; never touch main." and only then "On success, submit exactly one terminal `mc complete <task> --run <run_id> --status worked ...`". Under the dispatch bug D1 is explicitly hypothesizing (the backstop exists precisely for the case where D2's filter fails), the sequence is: Worker spawns on an unreviewed child → commits its changes to the shared initiative branch → calls `mc complete --status worked` → the trigger ABORTs. The committed child work already exists on the branch. The trigger prevented the *record*, not the commit.

4. So D1's sentence overstates. "A bug in `mc dispatch` cannot produce *committed child work* ... that the Editor never approved" is false as literal text; the true and still-valuable statement is that the spine can never record a `seeded → worked` advance on an unreviewed child. §4's own framing is correspondingly narrower than D1's: "A bug in `mc` cannot write an illegal state, because the storage rejects it" — states, not git side-effects. Every row in that table is a storage predicate; D1 asks to add a row to it while justifying it with a guarantee about the filesystem.

5. Scope check — this is a wording defect, not a design hole, and the reviewer is right to hold it at info. The blast radius is contained by Inv. 25's topology, quoted: "An initiative gets **one branch and one shared worktree** ... Merging into main always requires an explicit operator approval". A wrongly-dispatched Worker's commit sits on the initiative branch; main cannot move without the operator. The design (gate in dispatch, fence in storage) is right; only the justification sentence claims more than the mechanism delivers.

6. Note the ADR is precise elsewhere on this exact point, which is why the D1 sentence reads as a slip rather than a misconception. Line 456 confines its Inv. 25 claim to the non-buggy path: "an unreviewed child was never dispatchable, so it has no worktree changes and no commit to revert" — true, because there the dispatch filter held and no container ever ran. D1 is the one place that extends the guarantee into the bug case, where the container did run.

I found nothing that refutes the claim: no text anywhere in the ADR, §4, §6.2, or Inv. 25 places the child's git commit inside the SQLite transaction, and worker.md places it strictly before the terminal call.

Suggested repair (one sentence, no design change): replace "cannot produce *committed child work* ... that the Editor never approved" with a storage-scoped claim — e.g. "a bug in `mc dispatch` cannot get an unreviewed child's `seeded → worked` advance *recorded*: the Worker's terminal aborts, the child stays `seeded`, and any changes it committed are stranded on the initiative branch where Inv. 25's approval gate still stands between them and main." That keeps the §4-table row honest (it is a storage predicate, justified by a storage guarantee) and preserves D1's real point.
findings.evidence:
ADR-020 D1 (docs/adr/020-initiative-wave-plan-review.md:118-125): "**The gate, in storage.** A new `children_work_requires_plan_review`: `BEFORE UPDATE OF status WHEN OLD.initiative_id IS NOT NULL AND OLD.plan_reviewed = 0 AND OLD.status = 'seeded' AND NEW.status = 'worked'` → ABORT. This is the redundant backstop doing exactly the job §4 assigns it: a bug in `mc dispatch` cannot produce *committed child work* (Inv. 25 — a child's changes commit in the same breath as its `seeded → worked`) that the Editor never approved. The dispatch-side filter (D2) is the policy; this is the fence."

ADR-020 D1, the table row it justifies (docs/adr/020-initiative-wave-plan-review.md:127-129): "**§4 table row** (add to the spec-mirroring rule list in the schema header): \"Wave children work only after the Editor's plan review | `children_work_requires_plan_review` trigger\"."

DECISIVE — mc/verbs/directives/worker.md (commit precedes the recorded advance): "Work only inside the assigned Worksource/worktree. For mutable work, finish with one committed branch state; never touch main." ... "On success, submit exactly one terminal `mc complete <task> --run <run_id> --status worked [--branch <branch>] [--outputs <report>]`."

spec §6.2 (the commit is outside SQLite's reach): "This is contract enforced by `mc`'s runtime and the role briefs, not by SQLite."

spec §6.2 (and it runs in a container, not in the transaction): "**Mission Control's git runs in-container, at the canonical mount path.** Every mutating git operation Mission Control performs on a Worksource — worktree add/remove, branch, commit, merge, including the landing (§7) — runs only inside a container at the fixed canonical mount path; Mission Control never mutates a Worksource from the host."

spec §6.2 (the "same breath" phrasing D1 leans on is a runtime contract, not atomicity): "A child's changes are committed, tagged with its task id, in the same breath as its Worker-stage completion: the Verifier verifies a commit, the packet cites a stable range, and a rare in-flight cancellation is one `git revert`."

spec §4 (the table D1 adds to is scoped to states, not side-effects): "**Make-impossible-states-impossible at the substrate.** A bug in `mc` cannot write an illegal state, because the storage rejects it" ... "the substrate guarantees every stored state is a legal one."

spec Inv. 25 (why the blast radius is contained — severity stays info): "An initiative gets **one branch and one shared worktree**, cut at promotion; every wave child executes inside it ... Merging into main always requires an explicit operator approval — the packet decision for a standalone task, the arc-packet decision for an initiative."

ADR-020 line 456-457 (the ADR is precise on the non-buggy path, showing D1 is a slip): "**Inv. 25 holds trivially** on the send-back path: an unreviewed child was never dispatchable, so it has no worktree changes and no commit to revert."
